Power Spikes II (1994) by Video System on Neo Geo was a beach volleyball game with anime-style super spike moves that could only be stopped by matching super digs.
Kizuna Encounter: Super Tag Battle (1996) by SNK was a two-on-two tag fighting game, Garou-style, one year before the KOF 97 introduced new team mechanics.
Matrimelee (2002) by Noise Factory/SNK Playmore was one of the final Neo Geo fighting games, released long after the home market had moved on to PlayStation 2.
Rage of the Dragons (2002) by Noise Factory on Neo Geo introduced the Duo-Team Battle where tagging partners during a combo extended the hit string.
Metal Slug 4 (2002) by Mega Enterprise/Playmore was the first Metal Slug not developed internally by SNK, following SNK's 2001 bankruptcy.
Metal Slug 5 (2003) by Playmore removed the zombie and mutation mechanics of earlier entries, focusing on faster enemy patterns and more missile-heavy vehicle sections.
Samurai Shodown V Special (2004) by Playmore on Neo Geo restored decapitation fatalities that had been removed from V's original Japanese release due to content concerns.
SNK vs. Capcom: SVC Chaos (2003) by Playmore on Neo Geo was unusual as it was SNK-developed despite the 'vs. Capcom' branding, and featured different play balance from Capcom's crossover entries.
KOF 2002 by Playmore on Neo Geo removed the Strikers system and returned to the three-on-three elimination format, becoming one of the most-played KOF titles competitively.
KOF 2003 by Playmore was the final Neo Geo KOF entry and introduced mid-match tagging — pressing the start button during a match could substitute a fresh fighter.
Last Hope (2007) by NG:Dev.Team was a commercial horizontal shooter released on Neo Geo after SNK discontinued official support — one of the last new Neo Geo cartridges published.
Doki Doki Panic (1987) by Nintendo was originally a Fuji Television promotional game; it was rebranded as Super Mario Bros. 2 for Western markets.
VS. Super Mario Bros. (1986) by Nintendo was a harder arcade version of the NES game featuring altered level layouts and increased enemy speed.
Vs. Dr. Mario (1990) by Nintendo brought the NES puzzle game to the arcade with a versus mode and a new 'Ugly' difficulty above the standard Chill/Medium/Hot.
Vs. Pinball (1984) by Nintendo used real-time physics simulation for ball movement on an ROM-based board, one of the first digital pinball simulations.
Vs. Excitebike (1984) by Nintendo was an arcade-only variant supporting two simultaneous players on split screen, unlike the single-player NES version.
Vs. Tennis (1984) by Nintendo was the arcade basis for Tennis on NES, supporting two players on separate cabinets linked together.
Wild Gunman (1984) by Nintendo used a light gun and projected film of actors playing gunslingers — one of the first arcade games to combine live-action footage with electronics.
Arm Wrestling (1985) by Nintendo was a side-scrolling game where players pushed a joystick left and right against on-screen characters in an arm wrestling competition.
Playchoice-10 (1986) by Nintendo was an arcade system allowing operators to choose 10 NES games to offer per cabinet, bridging home and arcade markets.
F-Zero AX (2003) by Nintendo/Sega on Triforce hardware was a futuristic racer with a Ghost recording feature and exclusive courses unavailable in the GameCube home version.
Mario Kart Arcade GP (2005) by Nintendo/Namco on Triforce hardware was the first officially licensed Mario Kart arcade game, adding item customization and cup photography.
Luigi's Mansion Arcade (2015) by Capcom/Nintendo converted the home game into a co-op light-gun shooter using a vacuum cannon controller.
Star Wars (1983) by Atari used vector graphics and digitized voice samples from the film — one of the first arcade games to feature authentic film audio.
Return of the Jedi (1984) by Atari followed the vector Star Wars game with raster graphics, changing the visual style to match the different tone of the third film.
Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom (1985) by Atari was a top-down scrolling action game and one of the first fully licensed Indiana Jones arcade games.
Discs of Tron (1983) by Midway was an environmental cabinet with a curved monitor and surround sound, simulating the disc-throwing arena from the 1982 film.
Tron (1982) by Midway featured four mini-games tied to the film and used a spinner control alongside a joystick for the light cycle segment.
Dragon Spirit (1987) by Namco was a vertically scrolling dragon-themed shooter where players could grow a second head by collecting power-ups, doubling firepower.
The Tower of Druaga (1984) by Namco was an early dungeon crawler in which players memorized the location of hidden doors and items — it had no in-game hints whatsoever.
Mach Breakers (Numan Athletics 2) (1994) by Namco was a sci-fi track-and-field game with button-mashing events using a futuristic cyborg sports theme.
Giga Wing (1999) by Capcom on CPS-2 introduced a reflect barrier that could deflect all bullets back at enemies, creating enormous score multiplier chains.
19XX: The War Against Destiny (1995) by Capcom on CPS-2 was a vertical shooter with selectable ships and a lock-on weapon making it one of the series' most accessible entries.
Dimahoo (2000) by Raizing/Capcom on CPS-2 featured a lock-on laser system and a scoring mechanic tied to grazing enemy bullets without taking damage.
Progear no Arashi (2001) by Cave on CPS-2 was the last game released on the CPS-2 board — a horizontal steampunk shooter with a lock-on weapon system.
Night Slashers (1993) by Data East was a horror-themed beat-em-up featuring a priest, a cyborg soldier, and a vampire hunter fighting their way through a zombie apocalypse.
Violent Storm (1993) by Konami was a beat-em-up set in a post-apocalyptic world and featured garbage bin throws, pipe weapons, and a controversial but praised difficulty curve.
Vendetta (Crime Fighters 2) (1991) by Konami was a four-player simultaneous brawler with a gritty urban theme and destructible scenery.
Wild West C.O.W.-Boys of Moo Mesa (1992) by Konami was a four-player beat-em-up based on the Archie Comics animated series featuring anthropomorphic cattle in the Old West.
Bucky O'Hare (1992) by Konami was a side-scrolling run-and-gun platformer based on the comic and cartoon where each rescued ally had a unique power contribution.
Asterix (1992) by Konami was a four-player beat-em-up based on the French comic characters, published exclusively in European arcades.
Haunted Castle (1987) by Konami was the arcade version of Castlevania, featuring Simon Belmont in a harder and more expensive-per-life version of the NES game.
Lethal Crash Race (1993) by Ecole Software was a top-down racing game with car destruction — reducing rival vehicles to nothing before reaching the finish line.
Fast Furious (2004) by Raw Thrills based on the film franchise was the first officially licensed Fast & Furious arcade racing game, using photorealistic car models.
Initial D Arcade Stage 4 (2006) was one of the first Japanese arcade games to require a card for all features — non-card play was severely limited.
Melty Blood: Actress Again (2008) by Ecole/Type-Moon was the final major 2D fighter released to Japanese arcades before the genre consolidated onto console and PC.
Under Night In-Birth (2012) by French Bread/Ecole was the last major arcade-first 2D fighter before the platform declined entirely, requiring NESYS network login for online versus.
Pump It Up Fiesta (2010) by Andamiro introduced the channel system allowing players to create curated playlists, influencing how later rhythm game cabinets managed content updates.
Dance Dance Revolution X (2008) by Konami introduced the Extended chart type with 18-19 rated steps, officially surpassing any previous maximum difficulty in the series.
Groove Coaster (2011) by Taito used a single-rail timing track with only left and right buttons plus a stick flick, reducing inputs to pure rhythm without positional complexity.
maimai (2012) by Sega used a circular screen surrounded by touch buttons arranged around the cabinet's inner wall, creating a 360-degree touch rhythm experience.
Sound Voltex (2011) by Konami added two analog knob controllers to a six-button layout, requiring players to spin knobs in sync with the music while pressing keys.
Nostalgia (2017) by Konami used a realistic weighted piano keyboard as the input device, targeting players with musical instrument experience.
Chunithm (2015) by Sega used a large touch-sensitive bar spanning the full cabinet width, requiring swipes, slides, and air gestures detected by infrared sensors.
Wangan Midnight Maximum Tune (2004) by Namco became one of Japan's longest-running card-based arcade racing series, with highway races and extensive car tuning persistence.
Battle Gear (1999) by Taito on Type X hardware was a Japanese arcade tuner racer where players could save car configurations on memory cards between sessions.
Virtua Golf (2001) by Sega on NAOMI used a real-feel golf club controller where swing speed, angle, and timing controlled shot power and direction.
Spikeout (1998) by Sega on Model 3 was a 3D beat-em-up supporting up to four players, featuring a charge system and environmental hazard interactions.
Dynamite Dux (1988) by Sega was a run-and-gun arcade game featuring two duck characters (Bin and Pin) fighting through surreal enemy-filled levels.
Altered Beast (1988) by Sega used the phrase 'Rise from your grave!' as its opening line — one of the most recognizable audio samples in arcade history.
Rad Mobile (1991) by Sega was notable for including a small Sonic the Hedgehog figure hanging from the rearview mirror — Sonic's first-ever appearance in any game.
After Burner II (1987) by Sega used the OutRun Super Scaler hardware and offered a sit-down deluxe cabinet with a hydraulic motion base that tilted during barrel rolls.
Space Harrier (1985) by Sega was the first game to use Sega's Super Scaler hardware for smooth sprite scaling at high speed in a third-person rail shooter.
Thunder Blade (1987) by Sega alternated between isometric top-down and behind-the-helicopter third-person perspectives in a helicopter combat game.
Galaxy Force II (1988) by Sega was a full 3D rail shooter in a large rotating cabinet and used the System 32 hardware capable of nearly 100,000 polygons per second.
Outrun 2 (2003) by Sega on Chihiro hardware featured licensed Ferrari cars and a heart attack mode where passengers reacted to the player's driving performance.
Hyper Driving (Sega, 1994) was an early 3D racing game on Model 2 hardware and was considered a tech demo for the board's polygon rendering capabilities.
Top Ranking Stars (Super Side Kicks 3) (1995) by SNK on Neo Geo was a top-down soccer game with stadium atmosphere sounds and national team rosters.
Baseball Stars 2 (1992) by SNK on Neo Geo was one of the most realistic baseball simulations available in arcade form, supporting full nine-inning games.
NAM-1975 at Neo Geo launch demonstrated that arcade and home software could be identical, a first for the industry — no features removed for the home version.
Puzzle De Pon! (1996) by Visco on Neo Geo was a clone of Puzzle Bobble where monsters launched colored balls instead of dragons blowing bubbles.
Panic Bomber (1994) by Hudson Soft on Neo Geo was a falling-block puzzle game starring Bomberman, where matching bomb icons triggered chain explosions.
Tetris (Neo Geo, 1993) by SNK was planned for the Neo Geo but was cancelled due to the Tetris license dispute — cartridge prototypes are among the rarest Neo Geo collectibles.
Shock Troopers 2nd Squad (1998) by Noise Factory on Neo Geo added a vertically scrolling overhead mode to the original game's top-down run-and-gun gameplay.
Spin Master (Mystic Warriors 2) (1993) by Data East on Neo Geo was a side-scrolling run-and-gun about a young agent collecting a yo-yo as a projectile weapon.
Sengoku 3 (2001) by Noise Factory/Playmore on Neo Geo allowed mid-stage character switching and used the largest character sprites in the series.
Alpha Mission II (1991) by SNK on Neo Geo was a vertical shooter where armor pieces could be collected and combined in flight for different attack configurations.
Prehistoric Isle 2 (1999) by Yumekobo on Neo Geo was a horizontal shooter set among dinosaurs and featured a grappling-arm mechanic to grab enemies as shields.
SVC Chaos on Neo Geo (2003) introduced mid-boss encounters before the final boss — an unusual structural choice that extended play time versus a straight ladder.
Bang Bead (2000) by Visco on Neo Geo was a VS puzzle game where players bounced a ball between dividers to hit the opponent's field — a unique mechanic among puzzle games.
Captain Tomaday (1999) by Visco was the last vertical shooter published for Neo Geo hardware, released the same year as several competing NAOMI titles.
Ironclad (Chotetsu Buhin Kuwasarettari) (1996) by Saurus on Neo Geo was a horizontal shooter praised for its detailed ship designs and fluid parallax scrolling backgrounds.
Pleasure Goal (1996) by Saurus/SNK on Neo Geo was a two-on-two indoor soccer game with power shots that literally set the ball ablaze upon impact.
